targetBehaviors.label: Hitting or slapping others
targetBehaviors.description: When your child hits, slaps, or makes contact with others in a way that could hurt
targetBehaviors.educationalNote: Hitting is often a way children communicate frustration, seek attention, or avoid demands. Understanding the 'why' helps us teach better alternatives.

targetBehaviors.label: Screaming or loud crying
targetBehaviors.description: Loud vocalizations, screaming, or prolonged crying
targetBehaviors.educationalNote: Screaming can serve many functions - getting attention, escaping tasks, or expressing emotions. It's important to teach calmer ways to communicate these needs.

targetBehaviors.label: Biting (self or others)
targetBehaviors.description: Using teeth to bite people or self
targetBehaviors.educationalNote: Biting can be dangerous and requires immediate attention. Often related to sensory needs or communication difficulties.

targetBehaviors.label: Throwing objects
targetBehaviors.description: Throwing toys, food, or other items
targetBehaviors.educationalNote: Throwing is common when children are frustrated or seeking attention. Teaching 'gentle hands' and giving appropriate outlets can help.

targetBehaviors.label: Running away or leaving safe areas
targetBehaviors.description: Running away from caregivers, leaving designated areas without permission
targetBehaviors.educationalNote: Elopement is a serious safety concern. Professional guidance is essential to create a comprehensive safety plan.

targetBehaviors.label: Self-injury (hitting self, head banging)
targetBehaviors.description: Hitting own head, banging head on surfaces, scratching or hurting self
targetBehaviors.educationalNote: Self-injurious behavior requires immediate professional intervention. It may serve communication, sensory, or escape functions.

targetBehaviors.label: Spitting
targetBehaviors.description: Spitting at people or in inappropriate contexts
targetBehaviors.educationalNote: Spitting often gets a strong reaction, which can reinforce the behavior if attention is the goal.

targetBehaviors.label: Destroying property
targetBehaviors.description: Breaking toys, tearing books, damaging furniture or belongings
targetBehaviors.educationalNote: Property destruction can be attention-seeking, escape-motivated, or sensory. Teaching appropriate ways to handle frustration is key.

targetBehaviors.label: Refusing to follow directions
targetBehaviors.description: Ignoring requests, saying 'no,' or not doing what's asked
targetBehaviors.educationalNote: Noncompliance is very common. Consider if directions are clear, age-appropriate, and if the child has the skills to comply.

targetBehaviors.label: Tantrums (crying, dropping to floor)
targetBehaviors.description: Prolonged crying, falling to the ground, full-body protests
targetBehaviors.educationalNote: Tantrums are a normal part of development but can become problematic if they're the main way a child communicates needs.

targetBehaviors.label: Grabbing items from others
targetBehaviors.description: Taking toys or objects from others without permission
targetBehaviors.educationalNote: Grabbing is common when children lack the language to request or negotiate. Teaching 'my turn, your turn' and requesting skills helps.

targetBehaviors.label: Pushing or shoving
targetBehaviors.description: Using hands to push people or move them out of the way
targetBehaviors.educationalNote: Pushing can be functional (clearing space, getting attention) or impulsive. Teaching personal space and gentle touch is important.

targetBehaviors.label: Scratching others
targetBehaviors.educationalNote: Scratching can be defensive, attention-seeking, or sensory. Understanding the trigger helps prevent it.

targetBehaviors.label: Pinching
targetBehaviors.educationalNote: Pinching often occurs when children want attention or to communicate discomfort but lack better ways to do so.

targetBehaviors.label: Resistance to daily routines
targetBehaviors.description: Fighting against bedtime, mealtime, hygiene routines, transitions
targetBehaviors.educationalNote: Routine resistance is very common. Visual schedules and offering choices can make transitions smoother.

targetBehaviors.label: Persistent whining
targetBehaviors.educationalNote: Whining can be a learned way to get attention or desired items. Teaching and reinforcing appropriate requesting is the solution.

targetBehaviors.label: Constant interrupting
targetBehaviors.description: Interrupting conversations, activities, or others' work
targetBehaviors.educationalNote: Children with language delays may not understand social timing. Teaching waiting and turn-taking skills helps.

targetBehaviors.label: Asking the same question repeatedly
targetBehaviors.description: Repeating the same question even after receiving an answer
targetBehaviors.educationalNote: Repetitive questioning can be anxiety-driven or attention-seeking. Providing a consistent response and teaching coping strategies helps.

antecedentStrategies.label: Use a visual schedule
antecedentStrategies.description: Show pictures or words of what's coming next throughout the day
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: Visual schedules help children feel more in control and reduce anxiety about transitions. They work best when reviewed together before activities.

antecedentStrategies.label: Offer limited choices
antecedentStrategies.description: Give 2-3 options so the child feels some control (e.g., 'red shirt or blue shirt?')
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: Choice-giving prevents power struggles and teaches decision-making. Keep choices simple and limited to avoid overwhelming the child.

antecedentStrategies.label: Give warnings before transitions
antecedentStrategies.description: Provide 5-minute, 2-minute, and 1-minute warnings before ending activities
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: Transitions are hard for many children. Advance warnings help them mentally prepare and finish what they're doing.

antecedentStrategies.label: Remove or reduce distractions
antecedentStrategies.description: Minimize background noise, visual clutter, or competing activities
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: Children with attention difficulties need clearer environments to focus. Reducing sensory input helps them regulate.

antecedentStrategies.label: Do preferred activities first
antecedentStrategies.description: Start with something the child enjoys to build momentum
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: Starting positive increases cooperation and builds rapport. Once compliance is strong, you can shift to 'first-then' (work then play).

antecedentStrategies.label: Use timers for transitions
antecedentStrategies.description: Visual or auditory timers show how much time is left in an activity
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: Timers make abstract time concrete. Many children respond better to a timer than to a person telling them to stop.

antecedentStrategies.label: Simplify instructions
antecedentStrategies.description: Give one-step directions in clear, simple language
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: Multi-step directions can overwhelm children with language delays. Break tasks down and add steps gradually as skills improve.

antecedentStrategies.label: Check physical needs first
antecedentStrategies.description: Make sure child isn't hungry, tired, or needs the bathroom
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: Basic needs drive behavior. A hungry or tired child will have much more difficulty regulating behavior and following directions.

antecedentStrategies.label: Provide sensory breaks
antecedentStrategies.description: Offer movement, deep pressure, or calming activities throughout the day
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: Many children need regular sensory input to stay regulated. Scheduled breaks prevent seeking sensory input through problem behaviors.

antecedentStrategies.label: Use visual supports
antecedentStrategies.description: Show pictures, symbols, or written words to support understanding
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: Visuals bypass language difficulties and provide permanent prompts children can reference. They're especially helpful for multi-step tasks.

antecedentStrategies.label: Establish predictable routines
antecedentStrategies.description: Keep daily activities in a consistent order when possible
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: Routines create safety and predictability. When children know what to expect, anxiety and behavior problems decrease.

antecedentStrategies.label: Give attention BEFORE problem behavior
antecedentStrategies.description: Provide frequent positive attention when child is behaving well
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: Many behaviors are attention-seeking. If children get regular positive attention, they're less likely to misbehave for attention.

antecedentStrategies.label: Reduce task difficulty
antecedentStrategies.description: Make tasks easier if the child is consistently struggling or refusing
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: If a task is too hard, noncompliance and frustration are natural. Success builds momentum, so meet the child where they are.

antecedentStrategies.label: Shorten activity duration
antecedentStrategies.description: Reduce the length of difficult or non-preferred activities
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: Building tolerance happens gradually. Start with short durations the child can handle, then slowly increase as success grows.

antecedentStrategies.label: Provide physical space
antecedentStrategies.description: Give the child more personal space or room to move
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: Some children need more physical space to feel comfortable. Crowding can trigger defensive or aggressive responses.

antecedentStrategies.label: Create calming environment
antecedentStrategies.description: Use soft lighting, quiet music, or calming scents
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: Environmental factors affect regulation. A calmer environment helps children stay calm and focused.

antecedentStrategies.label: Offer fidget tools
antecedentStrategies.description: Provide stress balls, fidget spinners, or other manipulatives
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: Fidgets can help children focus and self-regulate. They're especially useful during seated or listening activities.

antecedentStrategies.label: Teach coping skills beforehand
antecedentStrategies.description: Practice calming strategies during neutral times before they're needed
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: Children can't learn new skills in the heat of the moment. Teaching and practicing coping strategies when calm is essential.

antecedentStrategies.label: Practice transitions when calm
antecedentStrategies.description: Role-play difficult transitions during non-stressful times
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: Rehearsing transitions builds the skill. Practice makes them feel familiar and less threatening.

antecedentStrategies.label: Schedule movement breaks
antecedentStrategies.description: Build in regular opportunities for physical activity
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: Movement is regulating for many children. Scheduled active breaks can prevent hyperactivity and impulsivity.

antecedentStrategies.label: Use 'First-Then' language
antecedentStrategies.description: 'First clean up, then snack' - shows what comes after the demand
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: First-then clarifies expectations and provides motivation. It shows there's a payoff for completing non-preferred tasks.

antecedentStrategies.label: Prime upcoming events
antecedentStrategies.description: Review what will happen before new or challenging situations
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: Priming reduces anxiety about unknowns. Talk through upcoming events, show pictures, or visit new places ahead of time.

antecedentStrategies.label: State clear expectations
antecedentStrategies.description: Tell the child exactly what behavior you expect before activities begin
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: Children can't meet expectations they don't understand. Be specific about what you want to see, not just what to avoid.

antecedentStrategies.label: Show token board before activity
antecedentStrategies.description: Review what the child is working for and how many tokens needed
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: Previewing the token system reminds children of the reward and motivates cooperation. Make sure the reward is truly motivating.

antecedentStrategies.label: Give rewards on a schedule (not just for behavior)
antecedentStrategies.description: Provide attention, breaks, or preferred items at regular intervals regardless of behavior
antecedentStrategies.educationalNote: If behaviors are attention-seeking or escape-motivated, providing these things regularly prevents the need to misbehave to get them.

teachingStrategies.label: Model the correct behavior
teachingStrategies.description: Show the child what to do by doing it yourself
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Children learn by watching. Modeling combined with practice is one of the most effective teaching methods.

teachingStrategies.label: Use positive reinforcement
teachingStrategies.description: Immediately praise and reward desired behaviors
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Reinforcement is the cornerstone of ABA. Catch your child being good and make a big deal out of it!

teachingStrategies.label: Practice during calm times
teachingStrategies.description: Teach new skills when the child is regulated and receptive
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Learning happens best in calm states. Don't try to teach new skills during meltdowns or high emotions.

teachingStrategies.label: Break skills into small steps
teachingStrategies.description: Teach one small piece at a time, gradually building the full skill
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Task analysis makes complex skills manageable. Celebrate small wins along the way to the final goal.

teachingStrategies.label: Use visual supports and demonstrations
teachingStrategies.description: Show pictures, videos, or demonstrations of what to do
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Visuals support language processing and provide a permanent model. They're especially powerful for children with language delays.

teachingStrategies.label: Role-play scenarios
teachingStrategies.description: Act out situations where the child can practice new skills
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Role-playing creates safe practice opportunities. Make it fun and engaging, not like a test.

teachingStrategies.label: Provide immediate feedback
teachingStrategies.description: Tell the child right away if they did it correctly or need to try again
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Timing matters in feedback. Immediate reinforcement or correction helps children make connections between behavior and consequences.

teachingStrategies.label: Use errorless teaching (prompts to ensure success)
teachingStrategies.description: Give enough help that the child almost always succeeds, then fade help
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Errorless teaching builds confidence and prevents frustration. Success is motivating and creates positive associations with learning.

teachingStrategies.label: Teach replacement communication
teachingStrategies.description: Teach words, signs, pictures, or gestures to replace problem behaviors
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Many problem behaviors are attempts to communicate. Teaching functional communication is often the most powerful intervention.

teachingStrategies.label: Use video modeling
teachingStrategies.description: Show videos of the desired behavior being performed correctly
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Video modeling is research-backed and highly effective. Children can watch themselves or peers performing the skill successfully.

teachingStrategies.label: Practice in natural settings
teachingStrategies.description: Teach skills in the real places they'll be used, not just at a table
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Skills learned in natural contexts generalize better. Teaching during real routines makes skills more functional.

teachingStrategies.label: Give specific praise
teachingStrategies.description: Say exactly what the child did well (e.g., 'I like how you asked nicely!')
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Specific praise teaches children what to repeat. Generic 'good job' doesn't tell them what they did right.

teachingStrategies.label: Use token boards or charts
teachingStrategies.description: Let the child earn tokens or stickers toward a bigger reward
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Token systems help children work toward delayed rewards. They make progress visible and keep motivation high during longer tasks.

teachingStrategies.label: Teach waiting skills
teachingStrategies.description: Practice waiting for short periods, gradually increasing duration
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Waiting is a critical life skill. Start with very short waits (5 seconds) and build up gradually.

teachingStrategies.label: Practice turn-taking
teachingStrategies.description: Use 'my turn, your turn' language and activities to build this skill
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Turn-taking is fundamental for social interaction. Make it fun with games, and provide lots of practice opportunities.

teachingStrategies.label: Teach problem-solving steps
teachingStrategies.description: Walk through: what's the problem, what can I do, what will happen if I do that?
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Problem-solving is a teachable skill. Use visuals and repetition to help children learn the process.

teachingStrategies.label: Use social stories
teachingStrategies.description: Create simple stories that explain expected behaviors in specific situations
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Social stories help children understand social expectations and consequences. They work best when reviewed multiple times before the situation.

teachingStrategies.label: Shape behavior gradually
teachingStrategies.description: Reinforce closer and closer approximations of the target behavior
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Shaping builds skills step by step. Reinforce progress toward the goal, even if it's not perfect yet.

teachingStrategies.label: Use chaining for multi-step tasks
teachingStrategies.description: Teach tasks in a sequence, either forward (first step) or backward (last step first)
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Chaining helps teach complex routines. Backward chaining often works well because the child experiences success (task completion) quickly.

teachingStrategies.label: Use peer models
teachingStrategies.description: Pair the child with peers who demonstrate the target behavior
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Peers are powerful teachers. Children often imitate others their age more readily than adults.

teachingStrategies.label: Reinforce alternative behaviors
teachingStrategies.description: Heavily reward behaviors that are incompatible with or alternatives to the problem behavior
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: You can't do two things at once. Reinforcing good behaviors increases them while indirectly decreasing problem behaviors.

teachingStrategies.label: Teach requesting (manding)
teachingStrategies.description: Teach the child to ask for what they want using words, signs, or pictures
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Requesting is the most functional communication skill. It gives children power over their environment and reduces frustration.

teachingStrategies.label: Teach through natural consequences
teachingStrategies.description: Let safe natural outcomes teach the lesson (e.g., no snack if they threw it)
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Natural consequences are powerful teachers, but only use them when safe. They help children understand cause and effect.

teachingStrategies.label: Use contrived (artificial) rewards initially
teachingStrategies.description: Start with tangible rewards like treats or toys, then fade to natural ones
teachingStrategies.educationalNote: Sometimes natural reinforcement isn't strong enough at first. Starting with bigger rewards helps build the behavior, then you can fade them.

consequenceStrategies.label: Planned ignoring (for attention-seeking)
consequenceStrategies.description: Stay calm and don't give attention when the behavior happens
consequenceStrategies.warning: ⚠️ Only use if behavior is safe to ignore (no injury risk)
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: Both positive (praise) AND negative (yelling, scolding) attention can reinforce behavior. Planned ignoring removes all attention, but expect an extinction burst (behavior gets worse before better).

consequenceStrategies.label: Redirect to appropriate behavior
consequenceStrategies.description: Calmly guide the child to do the correct behavior instead
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: Redirection teaches what TO do, not just what not to do. Stay calm and matter-of-fact when redirecting.

consequenceStrategies.label: Allow natural consequences (when safe)
consequenceStrategies.description: Let the natural outcome occur if it's safe (e.g., toy breaks if thrown)
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: Natural consequences teach cause and effect without you being the 'bad guy.' Only use when consequences are safe and immediate.

consequenceStrategies.label: Stay calm with neutral tone
consequenceStrategies.description: Keep your voice even and emotion out of your face
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: Your reaction can reinforce behavior. Stay boring and calm so the child doesn't get the 'payoff' they're seeking.

consequenceStrategies.label: Brief removal of preferred items (30-60 seconds)
consequenceStrategies.description: Take away the toy or activity for a very short time, then return it
consequenceStrategies.warning: ⚠️ Keep removals brief - long timeouts can escalate behavior
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: Short removals can work for behaviors related to toys or activities. The key is brevity and consistency.

consequenceStrategies.label: Provide a break area
consequenceStrategies.description: Offer a calm space where the child can regulate (not as punishment)
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: Breaks are for regulation, not punishment. Teaching children to recognize when they need a break is a valuable skill.

consequenceStrategies.label: Practice correct response after behavior
consequenceStrategies.description: Have the child practice the right way to handle the situation
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: Practicing the correct response is a teaching moment. It builds the skill while providing a mild consequence.

consequenceStrategies.label: Immediately reinforce alternative behaviors
consequenceStrategies.description: When the child uses the replacement behavior, respond immediately with attention/rewards
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: The replacement behavior needs to work BETTER than the problem behavior. Make sure it gets a strong, immediate response.

consequenceStrategies.label: Stay consistent with your response
consequenceStrategies.description: Respond the same way every time the behavior happens
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: Inconsistency makes behaviors worse. The child keeps trying if the behavior works sometimes.

consequenceStrategies.label: Avoid arguing or lecturing
consequenceStrategies.description: Don't engage in back-and-forth or give long explanations
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: Arguments and lectures are attention, which can reinforce behavior. Keep responses brief and redirect.

consequenceStrategies.label: Prompt correct behavior and reinforce
consequenceStrategies.description: Help the child do it right, then praise them for doing it
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: Even if you had to help, reinforce the end result. This builds the association between correct behavior and positive outcomes.

consequenceStrategies.label: Use 'do-over' practice
consequenceStrategies.description: Have the child try again the right way immediately after the behavior
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: Do-overs give immediate practice of the correct skill. They teach rather than punish.

consequenceStrategies.label: Provide a sensory alternative
consequenceStrategies.description: If behavior is sensory-seeking, offer an appropriate replacement (chewy, fidget, etc.)
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: If behavior serves a sensory function, the child needs that input. Provide it in a safe, appropriate way.

consequenceStrategies.label: Block the behavior and redirect
consequenceStrategies.description: Physically prevent the behavior if needed, then redirect to appropriate action
consequenceStrategies.warning: ⚠️ Only use safe physical blocking - consult a professional for aggressive behaviors
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: Blocking prevents injury while redirection teaches. Always pair blocking with teaching the right way.

consequenceStrategies.label: Remove audience if attention-seeking
consequenceStrategies.description: Have others leave the area if the behavior is for their attention
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: Some behaviors are to get peer or sibling attention. Removing the audience removes the payoff.

consequenceStrategies.label: Prompt functional communication
consequenceStrategies.description: Help the child request or communicate appropriately, then honor it
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: Prompting communication and honoring it teaches that communication works better than problem behavior.

consequenceStrategies.label: Response cost (lose a token/privilege)
consequenceStrategies.description: Remove one token from a token board or lose a small privilege
consequenceStrategies.warning: ⚠️ Use sparingly and always pair with opportunities to earn back rewards
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: Response cost can work if there's also lots of opportunity to earn. Don't make it impossible to succeed.

consequenceStrategies.label: Logical consequence related to behavior
consequenceStrategies.description: Consequence is directly related to the behavior (threw toy → lose toy for a bit)
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: Logical consequences make sense and help children understand the connection between actions and outcomes.

consequenceStrategies.label: Active ignoring (attend to others nearby)
consequenceStrategies.description: Don't acknowledge the behavior but give attention to others who are behaving appropriately
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: Active ignoring shows what DOES get attention. It's particularly powerful in group settings.

consequenceStrategies.label: Use time-based reinforcement schedule
consequenceStrategies.description: Provide rewards based on intervals without the behavior (e.g., every 5 minutes of no hitting)
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: Time-based reinforcement rewards the absence of behavior. It builds duration of appropriate behavior.

consequenceStrategies.label: Reinforce lower rates of behavior
consequenceStrategies.description: Reward periods where the behavior happens less often (e.g., only 2 times instead of 10)
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: You don't have to wait for zero occurrences. Reinforce improvement and gradually raise the bar.

consequenceStrategies.label: Have child participate in repairing
consequenceStrategies.description: If something broke or someone got hurt, child helps fix or make amends
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: Repairing teaches responsibility and empathy. It's a natural consequence that also teaches skills.

consequenceStrategies.label: Minimal verbal attention during behavior
consequenceStrategies.description: Use few or no words when responding to the behavior
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: Talking can be reinforcing. Keep verbal responses minimal and boring during problem behaviors.

consequenceStrategies.label: Continue the demand (if behavior is escape-motivated)
consequenceStrategies.description: Don't remove the task when the behavior happens - wait for compliance
consequenceStrategies.warning: ⚠️ This can escalate behaviors initially - consult a BCBA for implementation
consequenceStrategies.educationalNote: If behavior is to escape demands, allowing escape reinforces it. Continuing the demand (safely) teaches that the behavior doesn't work.

replacementBehaviors.label: Ask for a break
replacementBehaviors.description: Request a break using words, signs, or pictures
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Teaching children to ask for breaks reduces escape-motivated behaviors

replacementBehaviors.label: Use words to express feelings
replacementBehaviors.description: Say 'I'm mad,' 'I'm sad,' or 'I'm frustrated'
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Labeling emotions is a critical skill for emotional regulation

replacementBehaviors.label: Request items appropriately
replacementBehaviors.description: Ask for desired items using words, signs, or pictures
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Functional requesting replaces grabbing, tantrums, and other access-motivated behaviors

replacementBehaviors.label: Raise hand for attention
replacementBehaviors.description: Raise hand and wait to be called on
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Hand-raising is a socially appropriate way to get attention

replacementBehaviors.label: Say 'help please'
replacementBehaviors.description: Request assistance when struggling
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Asking for help prevents frustration-based behaviors

replacementBehaviors.label: Use a calming strategy
replacementBehaviors.description: Deep breathing, counting, or using a calm-down tool
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Self-calming strategies must be taught and practiced when calm

replacementBehaviors.label: Take deep breaths
replacementBehaviors.description: Use deep breathing techniques to calm down
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Deep breathing is evidence-based for emotional regulation

replacementBehaviors.label: Count to 10
replacementBehaviors.description: Pause and count to calm down before reacting
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Counting provides a pause that prevents impulsive reactions

replacementBehaviors.label: Go to calm-down space
replacementBehaviors.description: Move to a designated quiet area to regulate
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Teaching children to self-initiate breaks is a powerful self-regulation skill

replacementBehaviors.label: Hold a fidget
replacementBehaviors.description: Use a fidget tool, stress ball, or sensory item
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Fidgets can help with focus and self-regulation

replacementBehaviors.label: Use gentle touch
replacementBehaviors.description: Touch others gently instead of hitting, pushing, or grabbing
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Teaching 'gentle hands' provides a physical alternative to aggression

replacementBehaviors.label: Wait patiently
replacementBehaviors.description: Stand or sit quietly while waiting for a turn
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Waiting is a critical skill that must be built gradually

replacementBehaviors.label: Take turns
replacementBehaviors.description: Use 'my turn, your turn' language and actions
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Turn-taking is fundamental for social interaction and play

replacementBehaviors.label: Share with others
replacementBehaviors.description: Offer to share toys or materials
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Sharing is a complex skill involving perspective-taking and delayed gratification

replacementBehaviors.label: Ask to join play
replacementBehaviors.description: Request to join others' activities appropriately
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Teaching entry skills prevents grabbing, pushing, or disrupting play

replacementBehaviors.label: Request sensory input
replacementBehaviors.description: Ask for pressure, movement, or sensory activity
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Teaching children to request sensory needs prevents sensory-seeking behaviors

replacementBehaviors.label: Ask for movement break
replacementBehaviors.description: Request permission to move, jump, or get energy out
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Movement breaks are regulating for many children

replacementBehaviors.label: Use sensory tools
replacementBehaviors.description: Choose appropriate sensory items (chewy, fidget, weighted item)
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Providing sensory alternatives prevents inappropriate sensory-seeking

replacementBehaviors.label: Walk away from conflict
replacementBehaviors.description: Remove self from frustrating or escalating situations
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Walking away is an appropriate escape that prevents aggression

replacementBehaviors.label: Use words, not hands
replacementBehaviors.description: Express needs verbally instead of physically
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: This simple phrase helps children remember to communicate instead of act

replacementBehaviors.label: Wait for an answer
replacementBehaviors.description: Ask once and wait without repeating
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Reduces repetitive questioning and interrupting

replacementBehaviors.label: Use inside voice
replacementBehaviors.description: Speak at an appropriate volume for the setting
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Volume control is important for social appropriateness

replacementBehaviors.label: Ask permission first
replacementBehaviors.description: Request before taking or using others' items
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Asking permission prevents grabbing and conflict

replacementBehaviors.label: Say 'excuse me'
replacementBehaviors.description: Politely get attention instead of interrupting
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Teaches socially appropriate attention-getting

replacementBehaviors.label: Accept 'no' calmly
replacementBehaviors.description: Acknowledge and accept when told 'no' without escalation
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Accepting denial is challenging but critical for emotional regulation

replacementBehaviors.label: Wait for my turn
replacementBehaviors.description: Wait without interrupting or demanding immediate access
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Turn-waiting builds patience and frustration tolerance

replacementBehaviors.label: Trade or negotiate
replacementBehaviors.description: Offer to trade items or negotiate for desired objects
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Negotiation is a sophisticated social skill that prevents conflict

replacementBehaviors.label: Look at speaker
replacementBehaviors.description: Orient body and face toward person talking
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Attending skills improve learning and social connection

replacementBehaviors.label: Sit in designated spot
replacementBehaviors.description: Sit in assigned area during appropriate times
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Sitting skills are important for classroom and group settings

replacementBehaviors.label: Follow directions first time
replacementBehaviors.description: Comply with instructions without delay or protest
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Compliance is foundational for learning and safety

replacementBehaviors.label: Tell an adult
replacementBehaviors.description: Report problems or needs to a caregiver
replacementBehaviors.educationalNote: Reporting replaces retaliation and helps children get needs met safely

settingEvents.label: Lack of sleep
settingEvents.description: Child did not get adequate or quality sleep

settingEvents.label: Hunger or thirst
settingEvents.description: Child is hungry, thirsty, or has low blood sugar

settingEvents.label: Illness or physical discomfort
settingEvents.description: Child is sick, in pain, or physically uncomfortable

settingEvents.label: Change in routine or schedule
settingEvents.description: Disruption to normal daily schedule or expectations

settingEvents.label: Overstimulation or sensory overload
settingEvents.description: Too much sensory input (noise, lights, people, activity)

settingEvents.label: Medication changes
settingEvents.description: New medication, dosage change, or missed dose

precursors.label: Body language changes
precursors.description: Physical posture or muscle tension shifts

precursors.label: Vocal changes
precursors.description: Changes in voice tone, volume, or speech pattern

precursors.label: Decreased compliance
precursors.description: Starts ignoring or refusing simple requests

precursors.label: Minor disruptive behaviors
examples: Touching things after being told no

precursors.label: Withdrawal or isolation
precursors.description: Pulling away from interaction or activities

precursors.label: Requesting escape
precursors.description: Verbal or non-verbal attempts to leave situation
